blocks.content: They had gone back to the doctor one final time.
They had asked the questions. They had sat in the car afterward and Cassandra had said: I don't want to spend whatever time I have waiting for it to end. And Ethan had said: Then we won't.

That was the decision. No device. No clinical vigil above and beyond what she had always practiced. Just the life — all the ordinary of it, taken with both hands, lived deliberately and without the weight of constant bracing.
The peace of it surprised them both.
The years settled over them the way good years do — not in a rush, but gradually, like light through a window you stop noticing because it's always there.

Harold was still on the shelf. More worn now — one ear slightly softer than the other from years of being held in quiet moments. He had the expression he had always had. The expression of something that had outlasted everything and intended to continue.

Ethan's work had grown. He had a proper studio now, a few blocks from the house, with north-facing windows and enough room to spread out. His name appeared in places it hadn't before. He was commissioned for things. He still drew her — had never stopped drawing her — but his world had widened in the years they had built together.

She was still beautiful. Not in the way she had been at nineteen — that particular brightness, that red-haired aliveness that had made him turn around on a park bench — but in the way a person becomes beautiful when they have lived fully and been loved fully and the evidence of both is written in their face.
